case_name: Mine drainage reclamation for irrigation with partially characterized organic solvents and elevated strontium
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for converting partially characterized mine drainage into water suitable for agricultural reuse under contaminant uncertainty and cost-energy constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A closed polymetallic mining area is collecting drainage in a holding pond and wants to reuse treated water for irrigation of non-food and salt-moderately-sensitive crops during dry months. Historical site records indicate legacy degreasing solvent use near maintenance shops and possible detergent-related alkylphenol inputs, but the current organic contaminant mixture is only partly characterized and appears to vary after rainfall events. The drainage chemistry also shifts seasonally because of blending between acidic seepage, neutralized runoff, and shallow groundwater intrusion, so both dissolved salts and trace organics are uncertain in magnitude. The owner prefers a robust treatment train that can handle variability without committing immediately to the highest-energy full desalination option unless justified for irrigation risk control.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should be suitable for agricultural reuse with reduced suspended solids, controllable salinity and hardness, and strong reduction of tetrachloroethylene, 4-nonylphenol, and dissolved metals while keeping irrigation soil and crop risks acceptable.
secondary_goals: maintain resilience under seasonal chemistry swings and storm-driven contaminant pulses
avoid recommending energy-intensive desalination unless justified by irrigation salinity and strontium risk
provide barriers for both volatile chlorinated solvent removal and hydrophobic trace organic removal
explicitly account for uncertainty in uncharacterized co-contaminants

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: metal-bearing sludge can be dewatered on site, but concentrated brine or high-volume spent regenerant disposal is logistically difficult and costly

core_unit_functions.equalization_and_blending: buffer flow and chemistry variability and dampen storm-related contaminant spikes
core_unit_functions.lime_or_caustic_pH_adjustment_with_oxida: precipitate iron and manganese, reduce suspended solids, and condition water for downstream adsorption and membranes
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: polish precipitated solids and reduce particulate fouling load
core_unit_functions.air_stripping_or_packed_tower_aeration_w: remove volatile tetrachloroethylene and transfer it to a controllable off-gas treatment system
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb 4-nonylphenol and residual dissolved organics and provide a polishing barrier for incompletely characterized trace contaminants
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_partial_reverse_osmosi: reduce hardness, sulfate, strontium, and part of TDS to improve irrigation suitability
core_unit_functions.stabilization_and_final_disinfection: adjust finished water chemistry for distribution and control microbial risk in reuse storage

applicability_rationale: this multi-barrier train matches the co-occurrence of volatile chlorinated solvent, hydrophobic trace organic, metals, and elevated divalent ions
equalization is important because influent variability is high and source blending changes seasonally
stripping is well suited for tetrachloroethylene while GAC is better aligned with 4-nonylphenol and other uncertain organic residuals
nanofiltration or partial RO is justified because sulfate, hardness, TDS, and strontium are elevated enough to create irrigation and scaling concerns even if full desalination may not be necessary
the train balances performance and energy better than using full RO as the primary removal method for all contaminants
potential_risks: off-gas from air stripping requires vapor-phase carbon or equivalent control, otherwise solvent is transferred rather than destroyed
nanofiltration or RO concentrate management remains a significant uncertainty under the stated residuals constraints
if the organic mixture includes non-volatile polar compounds not captured well by stripping or GAC, additional polishing may be needed
rank assumes irrigation targets can be met with partial desalination; if crop sensitivity or soil constraints are tighter than stated, higher desalting duty may be required

core_unit_functions.equalization: stabilize variable mine drainage quality and provide operational buffering
core_unit_functions.chemical_oxidation_and_high_rate_clarifi: convert and remove iron and manganese and lower suspended solids
core_unit_functions.media_filtration: protect adsorption and ion-exchange stages by polishing solids
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: remove 4-nonylphenol and part of the uncertain organic mixture
core_unit_functions.strong_acid_cation_exchange_or_selective: target strontium and part of hardness reduction without full membrane desalination
core_unit_functions.final_disinfection: provide microbial control for storage and reuse distribution

applicability_rationale: this option reduces energy demand relative to membrane desalination and directly addresses elevated strontium through exchange media
it is attractive where brine disposal is difficult and salinity targets are moderate rather than stringent
GAC provides a practical hedge against uncertain hydrophobic organics that are not fully characterized
it can be a reasonable compromise if tetrachloroethylene concentrations are intermittent and low enough to be handled by GAC polishing, but this is less certain than dedicated stripping
potential_risks: tetrachloroethylene removal is less robust than in rank 1 unless a dedicated VOC removal step is added
ion exchange generates regenerant waste and performance can be impaired by competing hardness ions and high sulfate conditions
does not substantially lower overall TDS, so suitability depends on crop and soil tolerance
uncertainty flag: if storm events raise VOCs or introduce additional dissolved organics, GAC life may shorten significantly

core_unit_functions.equalization_and_settling: buffer variable flows and remove coarse suspended matter
core_unit_functions.neutralization_with_metal_precipitation: control pH and remove precipitable metals before membranes
core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: provide a stable low-solids feed to desalination
core_unit_functions.air_stripping_with_offgas_treatment: remove volatile tetrachloroethylene to reduce RO exposure and emissions risk
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: strongly reduce TDS, sulfate, hardness, strontium, and many dissolved contaminants
core_unit_functions.final_disinfection: protect reuse water during storage and conveyance

applicability_rationale: this train offers the most comprehensive dissolved contaminant reduction and is technically strong if irrigation demands tighter salinity control than initially assumed
it is especially relevant if strontium reduction targets become stringent or if sulfate and hardness must be substantially lowered
air stripping before RO is sensible because relying on RO alone for volatile solvent management is not ideal
however, the option is ranked below the top choice because energy use and concentrate management are more burdensome under the stated constraints
potential_risks: RO concentrate disposal may be the limiting factor for implementation
membrane scaling and fouling risk is significant in sulfate-hardness mine drainage without careful antiscalant control and recovery management
the train may over-treat if agricultural reuse tolerates moderate salinity, creating avoidable cost and energy burden
uncertainty flag: if trace organics include compounds poorly rejected or causing fouling, additional carbon polishing may still be needed

core_unit_functions.neutralization_and_clarification: remove acidity-related precipitates and metal solids
core_unit_functions.constructed_wetland_or_biologically_acti: provide additional solids and some metals polishing with lower energy input
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb 4-nonylphenol and part of the residual organic load

applicability_rationale: this lower-energy route may appeal where funding and operator capacity are constrained and where the irrigation use is tolerant of moderate salinity
it can improve water quality substantially with simpler operation than membrane-heavy trains
GAC remains necessary because passive or biologically active polishing alone is not dependable for the stated organic uncertainty
potential_risks: it is unlikely to adequately reduce strontium, sulfate, hardness, and TDS for all agricultural scenarios
tetrachloroethylene removal is uncertain without a dedicated volatilization or destruction step
wetland or biofiltration performance can vary seasonally and may be less robust during shock events
included as a conditional option only if site-specific irrigation tolerance is permissive and confirmed by pilot testing

core_unit_functions.neutralization: adjust pH and promote precipitation of some metals

applicability_rationale: included as a low-ranked baseline conventional train
it may improve aesthetics and some metal-related parameters but does not address the full dissolved contaminant and trace organic challenge
potential_risks: insufficient for reliable tetrachloroethylene and 4-nonylphenol control
does not meaningfully reduce strontium, sulfate, hardness, or TDS
high risk of producing irrigation water that remains problematic for soils or crops
not robust against the stated uncertainty in legacy and emerging co-contaminants

preferred_rank_pattern: multi-barrier trains combining pretreatment, targeted organic control, and partial desalination or divalent-ion reduction should rank above simple clarification-only options
equalization should appear in higher-ranked solutions because influent variability and storm-driven contaminant pulses are explicit
solutions that distinguish between VOC control for tetrachloroethylene and adsorption-oriented control for 4-nonylphenol should rank higher than generic single-step organic treatment trains

must_reflect: mine drainage chemistry requires pH conditioning and solids or metals pretreatment before advanced polishing
agricultural reuse does not automatically require maximum desalination, so salinity control should be balanced against energy and concentrate constraints
elevated strontium should be recognized as a dissolved-ion issue not solved by conventional clarification
cost-energy-residuals tradeoffs should be explicitly discussed, especially for membrane and ion-exchange options

must_contain: why tetrachloroethylene often benefits from air stripping or other VOC-specific control rather than relying only on conventional solids removal
why 4-nonylphenol and incompletely characterized hydrophobic organics justify activated carbon or comparable polishing
why sulfate, hardness, TDS, and strontium can matter for irrigation even when sodium hazard is not extreme
why simple neutralization and filtration alone are insufficient for this partially characterized contaminant mixture

should_mention: uncertainty in the uncharacterized solvent and semi-volatile organic fraction and the need for confirmatory monitoring or pilot testing
storm-event and seasonal blending effects that can alter both VOC loading and dissolved salt composition
air-stripping off-gas management as a transfer-versus-treatment risk for tetrachloroethylene
membrane scaling, fouling, and concentrate disposal risk if NF or RO is selected
the possibility that agricultural suitability depends strongly on crop type, irrigation method, and soil accumulation risk rather than only on average water chemistry
